plainMeaning: Among two more kinds of palace-dwelling gods — the 'gold' kumāras and the 'wind' kumāras — stand seventy-two lakh and ninety-six lakh Jina-temples, and you can almost hear the gods' music ringing through them.
recitation: sovana-kumāra majha lākha, bahuṁtera prāsāda; channuṁ lākha vāyu majha, suṇiye sura-nāda

wordByWord.gloss: the Suvarṇakumāra (Suparṇakumāra) gods — one of the ten classes of mansion-dwelling (bhavanapati) deities
wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati/Apabhraṁśa sovaṇa < Skt. suvarṇa ('gold') + kumāra ('youthful god'); a recognized bhavanapati class (also called suparṇa-kumāra).
wordByWord.notes: Here the class-name stands by metonymy for 'the realm of the Suvarṇakumāra gods'.

wordByWord.gloss: within, among, in the midst of
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit majjha < Skt. madhya ('middle, within'); here a postposition governing the preceding class-name.
Skt. lakṣa via Prakrit lakkha; the standard South-Asian unit of 100,000.
wordByWord.notes: Functions here as the multiplier of the count that follows.

wordByWord.gloss: temple, shrine, lofty edifice (here a Jina-temple)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. prāsāda, 'a palace, temple, or stately building' (root pra-√sad).
wordByWord.notes: Synonym in this hymn for jina-caitya / jinhar; the eternal Jina-temples of the bhavanapati realm.
Distinct from the hymn's other 'chhannu' — the ninety-six Jinas; here it counts temples, not Jinas.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. lakṣa via Prakrit lakkha (second occurrence).
wordByWord.notes: Multiplies the ninety-six for the Vāyukumāra count.

wordByWord.gloss: the Vāyukumāra ('wind') gods — a class of mansion-dwelling deities
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. vāyu ('wind, air'); clipped form of vāyu-kumāra, the suffix -kumāra understood metri causa.
wordByWord.notes: Parallel to the preceding verse's clipped nāga (Nāgakumāra).

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit majjha < Skt. madhya; postposition governing vāyu.

wordByWord.gloss: (one) hears / let it be heard / listen!
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit suṇa-/suṇijjai < Skt. √śru ('to hear'); old Gujarati passive-imperative coloring.
wordByWord.notes: A devotional interjection breaking the enumeration; addressed to the reciter.

wordByWord.gloss: the sound/music of the gods, celestial resonance
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. sura ('god, deva') + nāda ('sound, sonorous tone'); a tatpuruṣa, 'sound-of-the-gods'.
wordByWord.notes: Evokes the divine bells, conches and song filling the heavenly shrines.

literalTranslation: Within the Suvarṇa(-kumāra realm), lakhs — seventy-two [lakh] temples; ninety-six lakh within the Vāyu(-kumāra realm); hear the music of the gods!
idiomaticTranslation: Within the mansions of the Gold-kumāra gods stand seventy-two lakh Jina-temples; ninety-six lakh more crown the realm of the Wind-kumāras — listen, how the music of the gods resounds through them all!

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: The dawn worshipper is in the middle of a temple-by-temple census of the bhavanapati (mansion-dwelling) gods of the lower world. The previous verse credited the Nāgakumāra with eighty-four lakh shrines; this verse adds two more classes — the Suvarṇakumāra ('gold' gods) with seventy-two lakh prāsādas and the Vāyukumāra ('wind' gods) with ninety-six lakh — while the next verse will name the remaining classes (Dvīpa, Diśā, Udadhi, Vidyut, Stanita, Agni). The figures are not Mūlā's invention but the fixed traditional reckoning of eternal (śāśvata) Jina-temples per class, which the poet simply distributes line by line. What lifts the couplet above a ledger is its final half-line, 'suṇiye suranāda' — 'hear the music of the gods!' Into an arithmetic of lakhs the poet drops a single sensory command, and the silent count suddenly fills with the bells, conches and celestial song that ring through those countless shrines. The two clipped class-names (sovana for suvarṇa-kumāra, vāyu for vāyu-kumāra, the -kumāra understood) keep the metre tight and the pace processional, one realm bowed to per breath. The repeated postposition majha ('within') frames each god-class as a vast interior space the worshipper mentally steps inside. Note that the 'chhannu' (ninety-six) here counts temples, not the famous ninety-six Jinas of the hymn's earlier dhālas — a deliberate near-echo that rewards an attentive reciter.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the śāśvata-caitya doctrine: Jina-temples and images are not merely built by human hands but exist eternally and uncreated throughout the cosmos — here in the adho-loka abodes of the bhavanapati gods on the Ratnaprabhā earth. That even these lower-order deities possess and honour crores of Jina-shrines makes a quiet doctrinal point: Jina-bhakti pervades every gati, every tier of existence, so the path's object of reverence is truly universe-wide. To enumerate temples one can never physically visit is itself bhāva-pūjā (worship in feeling): the count becomes a mental circumambulation of the whole lower world, and bowing to these eternal shrines accrues merit and steadies samyaktva (right faith).
elaboration.practicalRelevance: While chanting, resist the urge to add the lakhs up. Let the closing 'suṇiye suranāda' do its work: pause, picture the celestial music swelling through seventy-two and ninety-six lakh shrines, and bow inwardly to them. The verse trains the reciter to convert dry numbers into awe — a daily exercise in widening the heart beyond the temple one actually stands in.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha-sūtra 4.1–4.11 (the four classes of devas, with the ten bhavanapati sub-classes)
Triṣaṣṭiśalākāpuruṣacaritra 2.2 (bhavanapati abodes in the adho-loka / Ratnaprabhā)
crossReferences.source: Hemacandra (tr. Helen M. Johnson, Gaekwad's Oriental Series)

sources.title: Bhavanapati — Jainism definition (Jaina Iconography; Triṣaṣṭiśalākāpuruṣacaritra 2.2)
sources.consultedFor: Confirming the ten bhavanapati classes (incl. Suparṇa-/Suvarṇakumāra and Vāyukumāra) and their placement in the lower world (adho-loka) on the Ratnaprabhā earth.

sources.title: Prāsāda — definitions (Sanskrit/Shilpashastra)
sources.consultedFor: Sense of prāsāda as 'temple, palace, lofty edifice', supporting its use here for a Jina-temple.

sources.title: Suvarṇa — definitions
sources.consultedFor: Root sense suvarṇa = 'gold' underlying the class-name sovana-kumāra / Suvarṇakumāra.

sources.title: Vāyu — definitions
sources.consultedFor: Root sense vāyu = 'wind, air' underlying the clipped class-name vāyu(-kumāra) and the sura-nāda imagery.

notes: 'મઝ' (majha) is read as Prakrit majjha < Skt. madhya, i.e. the postposition 'within/among' (= Guj. māṁhe); it is not the pronoun 'my'. 'વાયુ' is a metrically clipped Vāyukumāra, the -kumāra understood by parallel with the preceding verse's nāga. 'બહુંતેર' = 72 and 'છન્નું' = 96 give the per-class totals of eternal Jina-prāsādas: Suvarṇakumāra 72 lakh, Vāyukumāra 96 lakh — consistent with the traditional bhavanapati reckoning (Asura 64, Nāga 84, Suvarṇa 72, Vāyu 96 lakh, and 76 lakh each for the remaining six, totalling 7 crore 72 lakh). These cosmographic figures are preserved as printed in the Anchalgaccha (Vidhipaksha) edition and are not independently audited. Spelling kept verbatim per the book's orthography.
